cards.text: A {{c1::floating point}} number is a number with a {{c2::fractional}} part (decimal point can 'float').

cards.front: What are the three floating point types in C++?
cards.back:
• float (4 bytes, ~7 digits precision)
  • double (8 bytes, ~15 digits precision)
  • long double (8-16 bytes, varies)

cards.text: Best practice: Favor {{c1::double}} over float unless space is at a premium, as double has better precision.

cards.question: What suffix makes this a float literal?
cards.explanation: Without the f suffix, 5.0 would be a double. The f forces it to be a float literal.

cards.text: By default, floating point literals are of type {{c1::double}}. Use {{c2::f}} suffix for float.

cards.question: Why might this print something unexpected?
cards.explanation: Many decimal numbers cannot be represented exactly in binary floating point, causing small rounding errors.

cards.text: Floating point numbers have limited {{c1::precision}} and may introduce small {{c2::rounding errors}}.

cards.concept: Why comparing floating point numbers with == is dangerous
cards.explanation:
Floating point rounding errors mean numbers that should be equal may differ slightly:
  • 0.1 + 0.2 might not exactly equal 0.3
  • Use epsilon comparison instead
  • Check if difference is less than small threshold

cards.front: What special values can floating point numbers have?
cards.back:
• Infinity (positive and negative)
  • NaN (Not a Number) - from invalid operations
  • -0 (negative zero)

cards.question: What does this code produce?
cards.explanation: Dividing a floating point number by zero produces infinity (not a crash or error like with integers).

cards.text: Use {{c1::std::setprecision()}} from {{c2::<iomanip>}} to control how many digits are output for floating point numbers.

cards.front: When should you use floating point numbers instead of integers?
cards.back:
When you need:
  • Fractional values (decimals)
  • Very large or very small numbers
  • Mathematical calculations requiring precision
Avoid for money (use integers for cents).

cards.text: To output floating point in fixed notation (not scientific), use {{c1::std::fixed}} manipulator.
